learning_objectives: Distinguish between delirium and dementia clinically.
Implement therapeutic interventions for cognitive disorders.
Recognize early signs of Alzheimer’s and related disorders.

sections.title: Introduction and Overview
sections.content: Cognitive changes in older adults often stem from delirium or dementia. This section explains why early recognition is vital.

sections.title: Understanding Delirium
sections.content: Delirium develops quickly and usually has a medical cause such as infection or medication effects. Symptoms fluctuate throughout the day.

sections.title: Managing Delirium
sections.content: Treat underlying triggers, maintain orientation cues, and involve family to calm and reassure the patient.

sections.title: Dementia Syndromes
sections.content: Progressive decline in memory and thinking occurs in Alzheimer’s and related disorders. Onset is gradual and irreversible.

sections.title: Alzheimer’s Disease
sections.content: Plaques and tangles in the brain lead to memory loss, language problems, and eventually loss of independence.

sections.title: Screening and Diagnosis
sections.content: Tools like the Mini‑Mental State Exam help differentiate delirium from dementia and track progression.

sections.title: Pharmacologic Options
sections.content: Cholinesterase inhibitors and memantine may slow decline, while antipsychotics are used cautiously for agitation.

sections.title: Non‑Drug Support
sections.content: Consistent routines, environmental cues, and caregiver education promote safety and quality of life.

sections.title: Ethical and Safety Considerations
sections.content: Decision‑making capacity, wandering risks, and caregiver stress require ongoing assessment and planning.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.content: Understanding cognitive disorders helps nurses tailor interventions and support families.
